title: Tight Lower Bounds under Asymmetric High-Order Hölder Smoothness and Uniform Convexity

reviews.summary: The paper establishes tight lower bounds on the oracle complexity of minimizing convex functions whose p-th order derivatives are Hölder-continuous with degree ν and parameter H, and that are uniformly convex with degree q and parameter σ, in two asymmetric regimes. For q > p + ν, it constructs a hard instance via an ℓ∞-ball truncated-Gaussian smoothing of a max-type nonsmooth function and proves a lower bound Ω((H/σ)^{2/(3(p+ν)-2)} (σ/ε)^{2(q-p-ν)/(q(3(p+ν)-2))}), matching the upper bounds of Song et al. (2021). For q < p + ν, it generalizes Nesterov’s chain construction to the Hölder-smooth and uniformly convex setting and proves a lower bound Ω((H/σ)^{2/(3(p+ν)-2)} + log log(((σ^{p+ν})/(H^q))^{1/(p+ν-q)} (1/ε))), also matching Song et al. (2021). The work unifies and extends prior lower bounds for first- and second-order cases to the general high-order Hölder-smooth and uniformly convex setting.
strengths: Introduces an ℓ∞-ball truncated-Gaussian smoothing operator tailored to avoid dimension dependence in smoothness constants while preserving locality; this addresses key shortcomings of uniform ℓ2-ball randomized smoothing in prior work.

Provides a careful indistinguishability argument across a sequence of hard functions using locality of the truncated smoothing, enabling standard adversarial trajectory constructions for high-order oracles.

Extends Nesterov’s hard-function framework to the joint Hölder-smooth and uniformly convex setting in the q < p + ν regime, generalizing results of Arjevani et al. (2019) and Kornowski & Shamir (2020).
While theoretical, the proofs are structured to match known upper bounds tightly, thereby validating optimality in both asymmetric regimes.
Clear mapping to existing results in special cases (e.g., p=1, q≥3) and to prior first-/second-order constructions provides strong sanity checks.
The high-level motivation for asymmetry (mismatch between oracle order p and uniform convexity q) is well-motivated with practical examples (e.g., cubic regularization, logistic regression).
The roles of smoothing locality vs. global kernels (Gaussian, softmax, Moreau) are discussed transparently, justifying the design choices.

Closes a notable open gap by providing tight lower bounds that match the general upper bounds of Song et al. (2021), thereby fully characterizing oracle complexity for a broad, practically relevant class.
The truncated-Gaussian-in-ℓ∞ idea is potentially reusable in other lower bound constructions where dimension-free constants and locality are both required.
weaknesses: Some steps in the smoothing analysis (e.g., proving dimension-free Lipschitz constants for high-order derivatives via TV distance bounds for translated truncated-Gaussian kernels) are only sketched and rely on nontrivial geometric/probabilistic arguments; certain constants and dependencies could be more rigorously justified.

The construction for q > p + ν is restricted to a bounded domain to ensure p-th order Hölder smoothness of the uniformly convex regularizer; while standard in lower bound proofs, clearer discussion of implications for global minimization settings would help.
The q < p + ν lower bound is stated to hold for deterministic/zero-respecting algorithms; randomized algorithms are not covered in that regime.

Some parameter selections (e.g., setting δ relative to the smoothing radii after p-fold smoothing) appear with typographic ambiguities (e.g., “δ = 2ρρ” vs “δ = 2pρ”), and should be clarified to remove doubt about the locality radius accumulation.

The passage from Lipschitz-smoothness (ν=1) of ∇^p to Hölder-ν continuity for general ν leverages bounded-domain arguments; explicit, rigorous tracking of ν, D, and ρ in the constant H would strengthen the presentation.

The manuscript contains several typographical errors, inconsistent exponents, and minor notation glitches (e.g., Hq vs H^q, v vs ν, subsection title “T ∈ Ω((H/σ)^{3(p+ν)/2-2})” vs the final bound “2/(3(p+ν)-2)”). These impede readability and verification.
Some lemmas are densely stated with many constants and parameters; a summary table of key parameters and their scaling would help readability.

While the related work is broad, explicit comparisons to the constants/assumptions in Doikov (2022) and Thomsen & Doikov (2024) for the q > p + ν, p=1 regime could better highlight what precisely is generalized or tightened.
A brief discussion on the feasibility of extending the q < p + ν lower bound to randomized algorithms (and obstacles due to lack of robust zero-chain structure) would be valuable.
reviews.questions:
1. In Section 4.2.3 (Step 7), you write “δ = 2ρρ” as the locality radius to ensure indistinguishability across Sρ composed p times. Should this be δ = 2pρ (i.e., support radius adds under convolution)? Please clarify the exact accumulation and provide a formal lemma on the support of Sρ^p.
2. Lemma 14 (iv): Could you provide a complete, dimension-free bound for the L1 distance between translated, ℓ∞-truncated Gaussian densities that leads directly to the constant “2/ρ” in the Lipschitz bound of ∇fρ? In particular, can you bound supd TV(φ(·−x), φ(·−x′)) ≤ c∥x−x′∥/ρ with an explicit universal c independent of d?
3. The transition from Lipschitz continuity of ∇^p under smoothing (ν=1) to Hölder-ν continuity (0<ν≤1) seems to rely on bounded-domain arguments. Could you explicitly state and prove the inequality that yields H = Θ(β/ρ^{p+ν-1}), tracking all dependencies on ρ, D, and ν?
4. For the q < p + ν construction, the bound in Section 5.2 header shows an exponent “3(p+ν)/2 − 2,” while the final derived rate is 2/(3(p+ν)−2). Is the header a typo? Please ensure consistency of exponents throughout.
5. Your results for q < p + ν hold for deterministic/zero-respecting algorithms. Can you comment on whether (and how) the construction could be adapted to randomized algorithms (e.g., robust zero-chains), or what fundamental obstacles prevent such an extension?
6. The function in the q > p + ν regime is defined on a bounded domain Q to guarantee p-th order Hölder smoothness of the regularizer. Could you discuss whether the lower bound extends to global domains (e.g., via appropriate cutoffs or barriers) without changing the rate, and what technical issues arise?
7. Minor but important: There are several typographical inconsistencies (e.g., Hq vs H^q in log-log terms, ν vs v, and some constants in Theorem 1’s final inequality). Could you provide a cleaned version with consolidated notation and a parameter table?
reviews.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and timely gap by providing tight, general lower bounds for high-order Hölder smooth and uniformly convex optimization, matching the comprehensive upper bounds of Song et al. (2021) across both asymmetric regimes q > p + ν and q < p + ν. The key technical innovation—ℓ∞-ball truncated-Gaussian smoothing—persuasively remedies the dimension-dependence and non-locality issues of prior smoothing-based lower bounds, while remaining compatible with high-order oracle models via locality. The generalization of Nesterov’s construction to the Hölder+uniformly convex setting is also valuable, and the separation between deterministic and randomized settings is stated clearly.

That said, the manuscript would benefit from tightening several technical arguments and fixing numerous typographical inconsistencies. In particular, fully detailing the dimension-free smoothing constant, clarifying the locality radius under p-fold smoothing, and presenting a precise derivation for the Hölder-ν constant would significantly strengthen confidence in the proofs. Given the significance and apparent correctness of the main results, and the novelty of the smoothing approach, I lean toward acceptance, contingent on addressing the noted clarity and rigor issues.
